title: Gay Relations in Persian Literature
description: Gay Relations in Persian Literature is a literary essay by Sirus Shamisa. The source page lists the work as banned in Iran.
descriptionParagraphs: Gay Relations in Persian Literature is a literary essay by Sirus Shamisa. The source page lists the work as banned in Iran.

The surviving record is interesting because it shows how even ordinary-looking books can acquire a charged political afterlife. As a literary essay, it can be read not only for subject matter but for the way form, tone, and circulation make a text feel dangerous, intimate, or politically usable to anxious officials.

It also matters as part of a wider censorship history in Iran. The present page is a dossier starter built from source-tracked ban records; the surviving note currently says: The source page lists the work as banned in Iran. More publication history, translations, and close reading can be added later.

overview.englishDescription: Gay Relations in Persian Literature is a literary essay by Sirus Shamisa. The source page lists the work as banned in Iran.
banningParagraphs: Gay Relations in Persian Literature entered censorship debates as a literary essay associated with controversy, publication history, and state scrutiny. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around political sensitivity.

The earliest event currently captured here is Post-1979 Islamic Republic period in Iran, where Iranian censors and the Ministry of Culture and Islamic Guidance banned or denied publication. The source page lists the work as banned in Iran. The source page lists the work as banned in Iran.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
